Facts.text: Alexios I Komnenos is crowned Byzantine emperor
University of Basel is founded in the Swiss Confederacy
Ignatius of Loyola becomes the first Superior General of the Jesuits
Maurice, Elector of Saxony begins an alliance with Russian Anikey Stroganov
Christian IV succeeds Frederick II as king of Denmark
Battle of Porto Farina: English fleet defeats Barbary pirates in Tunis
The miraculous statue entitled the Infant of Prague is solemnly crowned by command of Cardinal Harrach
Anthonie van der Heim is installed as Grand Pensionary of Holland
Congress decides on the design of the US flag: 13 red and white stripes and 20 stars
Casparus van Wooden patents chocolate milk powder in Amsterdam
St Etienne-Lyons railway fully opens in France
City of Los Angeles incorporated
Bryant's Minstrels debut "Dixie" in New York City in the finale of a blackface minstrel show
Opera "Dinorah" is produced in Paris
Peninsular Campaign, launched in southeastern Virginia, aimed at capturing Richmond during US Civil War begins, Confederate victory (US Civil War)
Siege of Yorktown, fought in York County and Newport News, Virginia begins (Yorktown campaign), inconclusive result (US Civil War)
US Civil War: Skirmish at Elkin's Ford (Little Missouri River), Arkansas
Ashmun Institute, in Oxford, Pennsylvania, is renamed Lincoln University
Golden Gate Park forms by City Order #800
Susanna Madora Salter elected 1st US woman mayor in Argonia, Kansas
Announcement of the discovery of gold in the Yukon
South Africa all out 35 vs England (Trott 4-19, Haigh 6-11)
British garrison of Reddersberg surrenders to Boer General De Wet
Earthquake in Kangra India, kills 20,000
Hugh Chalmers, automaker, suggests idea of baseball MVP
Army fires on striking mine workers at Lena-gold fields in Siberia
Chinese republic proclaimed in Tibet
Greek aviator Emmanuel Argyropoulos becomes the first pilot victim of the Hellenic Air Force when his plane crashes
Film serial "The Perils of Pauline" is shown for the first time in Los Angeles, California
Germany protests vigorously to the US, claiming it must insist that Britain lifts its blockade and assert American neutrality
US Senate agrees (82-6) to participate in WWI
Food riot in Amsterdam
Arabs attack Jews in Jerusalem
Stanley Cup Final, Denman Arena, Vancouver, BC: Ottawa Senators (NHL) beat Vancouver Millionaires (PCHA), 2-1 for a 3-2 series victory
WAAB (Baton Rouge, Louisiana) becomes 1st US radio station with "W" calls
Warner Bros. Pictures, Inc. is formally incorporated by siblings Jack L. Warner, Harry Warner, Sam Warner and Albert Warner, in Burbank, California
Greek dictator Theodoros Pangalos elected president
1st AAU Greco-Roman wrestling championships held
England cricket batsmanAndy Sandhamends Day 2 of 4th Test against West Indies in Kingston, Jamaica unbeaten on 309; 1st triple century in Test history; out for 325
Les Ames makes the 1st Test Cricket century by a wicketkeeper (149)
The Communist Party of Panama is founded
Charles G. King, University of Pittsburgh isolates Vitamin C, using adrenal samples provided by Hungarian Albert Szent-Györgyi

USS Akron dirigible crashes into Atlantic Ocean, off coast of New Jersey, in stormy weather; 73 die including US Navy Rear Admiral William A. Moffett - a major proponent of the airship fleet, there were 3 survivors
Faisal II ascends to throne of Iraq
German troops conquer Benghazi, Libya

WWII: US Army Air Force bomber 'Lady Be Good' fails to return to
 Soluch, Libya base after its maiden flight; presumed lost at sea, wreck was discovered in desert 15 years later
Allied Bucharest bombings targeting railroads kills 5,000
British troops capture Addis Ababa, Ethiopia
The Holocaust: US forces liberate the Ohrdruf concentration camp in Germany, the first such camp to be liberated by the US Army
World War II: Soviet forces liberate Hungary from German occupation, establishing their own communist satellite state. This was celebrated as Liberation Day until 1989.
World War II: US Army occupies Bielefeld in north-eastern Germany
Largest group of sunspots on record
United Nation's Convention on International Civil Aviation  forms the International Civil Aviation Organization
Israel & Jordan sign armistice agreement
The North Atlantic Treaty is signed in Washington, D.C., establishing the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), a military alliance between Western nations
WKRC TV channel 12 in Cincinnati, OH (ABC) begins broadcasting
Dirk Stikker becomes chairman of OES
KFDA TV channel 10 in Amarillo, TX (CBS) begins broadcasting
British government signs military treaty with Iraq
Enid Bagnold's "Chalk Garden" premieres in London
Heitor Villa-Lobos' 10th Symphony premieres French Radio National Orchestra, conducted by the composer, in Paris
First march against nuclear weapons is held from London to Aldermaston in England, home of the Ministry of Defence's Atomic Weapons Establishment
The CND Peace Symbol displayed in public for the first time in London
Mali Federation founded, consisting of Senegal & French Sudan (dissolved 1960)
Oscar awarded to Dutch director Bert Haanstra for documentary "Glass"
Project Ozma begins at Green Bank radio astronomy center
Senegal declares independence from France
Deposed President João Goulart leaves Brazil under threat of arrest, moves into life of exile in Uruguay, and later Argentina
the Beatles' "Can't Buy Me Love" single goes #1 and stays #1 for 5 weeks
The first model of the new Saab Viggen fighter aircraft plane is unveiled.
Pirate Radio Scotland changes name to Radio Ireland
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Amsterdam Marines chase "Nozems" or rebellious youth out of Central Station
Dutch De Young government forms
Apollo 6 launched atop Saturn V; unmanned
CBS-TV abruptly cancels "The Smothers Brothers Comedy Hour" three weeks after renewing it, citing the program's failure to provide advance screening for review before airing
Haskell Karp receives the 1st temporary artificial heart, implanted by surgeon Denton Cooley at Texas Heart Institute in Houston
Firestone World Tournament of Champions won by Don Johnson
Marine clay under houses liquefies in St-Jean-Vianney, Quebec, 31 die
1st electric power plant fueled by garbage begins operating
World Trade Center opens in New York as the world's tallest building at 110 stories, later destroyed in the 9/11 terrorist attacks
138 killed as a USAF plane evacuating Vietnamese orphans crashes on approach during an emergency landing at Tân Sơn Nhứt Air Base in South Vietnam
British sitcom "The Good Life" starring Richard Briers, Felicity Kendal, Paul Eddington and Penelope Keith debuts on BBC One
Seni Pramoj's Democratic Party wins elections in Thailand
Ex-President Zulfikar Ali Bhutto of Pakistan is executed.
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Henry Cisneros becomes the first Mexican-American to serve as mayor of a major U.S. city, San Antonio
6th NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Challenger 1 launches
Michael Frayn's "Benefactors" premieres in London
Tulane University cancels its basketball season amidst scandal
Dow Jones Industrial Average up 69.89 points, ending at record 2,390.34 points
Eddie Hill becomes the world's first driver to cover the quarter mile in under 5 seconds
Largest crowd (55,438) at an MLB Opening Day game at Cincinnati's Riverfront Stadium; Reds win over St. Louis Cardinals, 5-4 in 12 innings
Last broadcast of British soap opera "Crossroads" on ITV
MLB New York Mets set Opening Day record with 6 HRs, in a 10-6 win over Expos at Montreal
"Marshall Chronicles" premieres on ABC-TV
American stockbroker and securities law violator Ivan Boesky is released from federal custody
King Baudouin of Belgium, a devout Catholic, steps down as monarch for 36 hours to avoid signing a bill legalizing abortion
Senator John Heinz of Pennsylvania and six others are killed when a helicopter collides with their plane over an elementary school in Merion, Pennsylvania.
Game 1 of Mayor Challenge - NY Yankees beat NY Mets 6-4 at Yank Stad
Sali Berisha becomes president and Alexander Meksi premier of Albania
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, GC at Desert Mountain: Tom Shaw wins his lone career major title by 1 stroke from Mike Hill
First game played at Jacobs Field, Indians beat Mariners 4-3 in 11 innings
KLM Saab 340B crashes at Schiphol Airport, Amsterdam, Netherlands, 3 killed
Largest Opening Day crowd at Yankee Stadium, 56,706
MLB Chicago Cubs Karl "Tuffy" Rhodes, hit 3 HRs in 1993 hits 3 HRs on Opening Day vs Mets starter Dwight "Doc" Gooden at Wrigley Field, Chicago
Netscape Communications founded as Mosaic Communications
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Austin, Texas on KJFK 98.9 FM
Anaheim Ducks clinch their 1st-ever playoff berth
Braves officially open Turner Field against Cubs
Second Defense Meteorological Satellite Program weather satellite is launched aboard a Titan II rocket
Space shuttle mission STS 83 (Columbia) is launched
NFL Europe (Formerly WLAF), kicks off season
The Angolan government and UNITA rebels sign a peace treaty ending the Angolan Civil War
Fifteen British Royal Navy personnel held in Iran are released by the Iranian president
Raid on Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints owned YFZ Ranch in Texas; 401 children and 133 women taken into state custody
Africa's tallest monument, the African Renaissance Monument, dedicated outside Dakar, Senegal, designed by Pierre Goudiaby to commemorate Senegal's 50th anniversary of independence
Boris Tadić, President of Serbia, resigns
Somalia's National Theater is struck by a suicide bomber killing ten people including the presidents of the Somali Olympic Committee and Football Federation
74 people are killed after an illegally constructed building collapses in Thane, India
9 people have been killed on an axe-murdering rampage in Chhattisgarh state, India
Poecilotheria rajaei, a giant tarantula with a 20cm leg span, is discovered in Sri Lanka
President of the World Bank, Jim Yong Kim, claims that climate change will lead to battles over water and food within the next five to ten years
Alibababecomes the world's largest retailer according to US Securities and Exchange Commission
Chemical weapons attack on Khan Sheikhoun, Syria by Syrian government forces kills more than 80 civilians
Pink Star diamond sets world record price of $71 million for a gem at an action in Hong Kong
21st Commonwealth Games open in the Gold Coast, Australia
Belgian Prime Minister Charles Michel apologises for the kidnapping of thousands of mixed-race children during colonial period in Burundi, DR Congo and Rwanda
Kathie Lee Gifford leaves NBC's "Today" show after 11 years
US Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints pledges to roll back anti-LGBT policies, including not baptizing children of gay parents
Economist Rodrigo Chaves elected President of Costa Rica defeating current president José María Figueres
Eon Musk buys 9.2% of Twitter stock, making him the company's largest shareholder
Ukrainian government begins a war crimes investigation after 410 civilians found killed after the withdrawal of Russian forces from around Kyiv
Finland officially joins NATO at a ceremony in Brussels, becoming its 31st member and doubling NATO's border with Russia
Rare 1938 copy of the comic (Action Comics No. 1) that first introduced Superman sells for a record US$6 million at action
ComedianRussell Brandis charged with rape and sexual assault by British police
TsarIvan IVgives the patent to colonize "the abundant region along the Kama River" of North-Russia to the fur trading Stroganov merchant family
Francis Drakeis knighted byQueen Elizabeth Iaboard his galleon, the "Golden Hind" at Deptford, England

English KingCharles IIissues the Declaration of Breda, promising a general pardon for crimes committed during the English Civil War and the Interregnum and religious toleration for all those who accepted him as King
English KingJames IIpublishes his Declaration of Indulgence, a step towards establishing freedom of religion in the British Isles
First US Congress begins regular sessions duringGeorge Washington's presidency at Federal Hall, NYC (ends 1791)
Battle ofRacławice, part of the Polish-Lithuanian uprising led byTadeusz Kościuszkowith a tactical Polish victory
An officer mutiny forces French Emperor Napoléon Bonaparte to abdicate for the first time in flavor of his son, who is not accepted by the Allies occupying Paris
Charles Darwinaboard the HMS Beagle reaches Rio de Janeiro
Vice PresidentJohn Tylerbecomes the 10th President of the United States after the death of President William Henry Harrison
GeneralRobert E. Lee's Confederate army arrives at Amelia Courthouse, Amelia County, Virginia
Russian TsarAlexander IIof Russia narrowly escapes an assassination attempt by Dmitry Karakozov in the city of St. Petersburg
Assassination attempt on Prince of Wales, later British KingEdward VIIwhen shot by Jean-Baptiste Sipido in protest over Boer war
Cecil Rhodesscholarship fund forms with $10 million
Sigmund Romberg andOscar Hammerstein II's operetta "The New Moon" opens at the Drury Lane Theatre in London's West End
George Bernard Shaw's comedy play "Too True To Be Good" premieres in NYC; runs for 59 performances
Richard RodgersandLorenz Hart's musical "Higher & Higher" premieres in NYC
French GeneralCharles de Gaulleforms new regime in exile, with communists
84-year-oldConnie Mackchallenges 78-year-oldClark Griffithto a race from home to 1st base; it ends in a tie
Dutch Prince Bernhard visitsArgentine President JuanandEva Perónin Buenos Aires
Cheryl Crane (14), daughter of actressLana Turner, stabs to death organized crime figure Johnny Stompanato, her mother's boyfriend, in self-defense; crime later ruled a "justifiable homicide"
Eugene Ionesco's play "Tueur sans Gages" (The Killer Without Reason) premieres in Darmstadt
32nd Academy Awards: "Ben-Hur",Charlton Heston&Simone Signoretwin
Stephen Sondheim's "Follies" opens at Winter Garden Theater, NYC; runs for 524 performances, then most expensive Broadway musical at the time
Hank Aaronties Babe Ruth's home run record by hitting his 714th in Cincinnati against Jack Billingham
Steve Milleris arrested for burning his girlfriend's clothes
Winston Smith inOrwell's novel "Nineteen Eighty-Four" begins his secret diary in defiance of the totalitarian government of Oceania
Wayne Gretzkysets NHL record with 213th point of season
"Always & Forever" second studio album byRandy Travis(Billboard Album of the Year 1988)
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's last NBA game in Seattle
NY YankeeTommy Johnties record of playing 26 seasons, his 287th win puts him 19th overall as Yanks beat Twins on opening day 4-2
SingerGloria Estefanreleased from hospital after her tour bus accident
Jury deliberations begin inNoriegacase
American actorTony Curtis(68) undergoes heart-bypass surgery
LA DodgerDarryl Strawberrybegins substance abuse treatment
Jack Mafounds Chinese internet companyAlibaba
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, GC at Desert Mountain: Graham Marsh of Australia wins his second Champions Tour major by 3 strokes fromLarry Nelson
24th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees:Jeff Beck; Little Anthony and the Imperials;Metallica; Run-D.M.C.; Bobby Womack; Wanda Jackson; Bill Black; DJ Fontana; and Spooner Oldham
German Nobel Laureate,Günter Grass, publishes controversial poem that claims Israel is plotting to wipe out Iran
Former US presidentDonald Trumppleads not guilty in a NY court to 34 felony counts of falsifying documents related to 2016 hush money payments to a woman alleging they had an extramarital affair
PresidentJoe Bidenwarns Israeli PMBenjamin Netanyahuthat the US could shift its policy if Israel does not immediately address humanitarian concerns in Gaza and work towards a ceasefire

George Clooneyand Grant Heslov's stage drama "Good Night, and Good Luck", adapted from their 2005 film, and starring Clooney as journalistEdward R. Murrow, opens at the Winter Garden Theatre, New York City for a limited run
South Korean PresidentYoon Suk Yeolis removed from office after the country's constitutional court rules to uphold the impeachment vote against him in December 2024

Washington Capitals left wingAlex Ovechkinscores two goals in a 5-3 win over visiting Chicago Blackhawks to tie Wayne Gretzky at 894 for the most career goals in NHL history, and passes Jaromir Jagr for most game-winners with his 136th
Riots break out in over 100 cities in the United States following the assassination of African-American civil rights activistMartin Luther King Jr.
US civil rights activistMartin Luther King Jr.is assassinated by James Earl Ray at the Lorraine Hotel in Memphis, Tennessee
Microsoft is founded as a partnership betweenBill Gatesand Paul Allen to develop and sell BASIC interpreters for the Altair 8800
Ambrosius Blarer, German reformer, born in Konstanz, Germany (d. 1564)
William Strachey, English writer (early history of the English colonisation of North America), born in Saffron Walden, Essex (d. 1621)
Edward Nicholas, English statesman (Secretary of State to Charles I and Charles II), born in Winterbourne Earls, United Kingdom (d. 1669)
Antoine Galland, French orientalist and archaeologist, born in Rolot, France (d. 1715)
Grinling Gibbons, Dutch-born British sculptor and woodcarver, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1721)
Joseph-Nicolas Delisle, French astronomer and cartographer, born in Paris (d. 1768)
Johann Evangelist Schreiber, Swiss Baroque composer, born in Arth, Switzerland (d. 1800)
Benjamin Kennicott, English churchman and Hebrew scholar, born in Totnes, Devon (d. 1783)
Francesc Morera i Cots, Valencian composer, born in Sant Mateu, Spain (d. 1793)
Wolfgang Nicolaus Haueisen, German organist, composer, and pedagogue, born in Oehrenstock, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1804)
Niccolo Antonio Zingarelli, Italian composer (Andromeda), born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1837)
Vincenc Mašek, Bohemian composer, born in Zvíkovec, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1831)
John Hoppner, English portrait painter, born in Whitechapel, London (d. 1810)
Pierre-Paul Prud'hon, French Romantic painter and draughtsman - allegorical paintings and portraits, born in Cluny, Saône-et-Loire (d. 1823)
Stephen Storace, English composer, born in London, England (d. 1796)
Nachman of Breslov, Ukrainian founder of Breslov Hasidut, born in Międzybóż, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1810)
Bettina von Arnim, German writer and composer of the Romantic era (This Book Belongs to the King), born in Free City of Frankfurt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1859)
Thaddeus Stevens, US Radical Republican congressional leader (Rep-R-Pennsylvania 1849-53 1859-68), born in Danville, Vermont (d. 1868)
Joseph Fischhof, Czech-Austrian pianist and composer, born in Bučovice, Moravia (d. 1857)
Benjamin Pierce, American mathematician and astronomer (Peirce's criterion for outliers), born in Salem, Massachusetts (d. 1880)
John Blair Smith Todd, American politician and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Lexington, Kentucky (d. 1872)
John Wilson Sprague, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in White Creek, New York (d. 1893)
Thomas Mayne Reid, Irish-American novelist (The Headless Horseman), born in Ballyroney, County Down, Ireland (d. 1883)
Charles Devens Jr, American lawyer, jurist, statesman and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Charlestown, Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1891)
Carl Wilhelm Siemens, German engineer and inventor (laid undersea cables), born in Gehrden, Hanover, Germany (d. 1883)
Robert Byington Mitchell, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Mansfield, Ohio (d. 1882)
Zénobe Gramme, Belgian electrical engineer (Gramme dynamo), born in Jehay-Bodegnée, Belgium (d. 1901)
Margaret Oliphant, Scottish novelist and biographer (Beleaguered City), born in Wallyford, Scotland (d. 1897)
Edward Cary Walthall, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 1898)
John Hughlings Jackson, English neurologist and epilepsy researcher, born in Yorkshire, England (d. 1911)
(Charles) Jerome Hopkins, American composer, pianist, critic, and musical education advocate, born in Burlington, Vermont (d. 1898)
Edouard Lucas, French mathematician (Lucas sequences), born in Amiens, France (d. 1891)

Hans Richter, Austrian-Hungarian orchestral and opera conductor who led  first complete performance of Richard Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen" at the Bayreuth Festspielhaus (1876), born in Raab, Kingdom of Hungary, Austrian Empire (d. 1916)
Comte de Lautréamont, French writer (Les Chants de Maldoror), born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 1870)
Raoul Pictet, Swiss physicist who invented liquid nitrogen and was a pioneer of cryogenics, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1929)
Tad Lincoln, youngest son of U.S. president Abraham Lincoln, born in Springfield, Illinois (d. 1871)
Remy de Gourmont, French poet, born in Bazoches-en-Houlmes, France (d. 1915)
George P Baker, American educator and playwright (47 Workshop), born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1935)

Felipe G. Calderon, Filipino lawyer, politician, educator, and co-author of Malolos Constitution (of the First Philippine Republic), born in Santa Cruz de Malabon, Cavite, Philippines
 (d. 1908)
Mary Colter, American architect and designer (Fred Harvey Company, Santa Fe Railroad), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1958)
George Albert Smith, American religious leader, 8th President of Latter-day Saints, born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 1951)
Nikolai Amani, Russian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1904)
Pauline de Haan-Manifarges, Dutch concert and opera contralto singer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1954)
Jozef Szulc, Polish composer, born in Warsaw, Russian Poland (d. 1956)

Pierre Monteux, French-American conductor  (Boston Symphony, 1919-24; Concertgebouw, 1924–34; Orchestre Symphonique de Paris, 1929–38; San Francisco Symphony, 1936–52, London Symphony, 1961-64), born in Paris, France (d. 1964)
Samuel S. Hinds, American actor (Raven, Test Pilot), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1948)
Maurice de Vlaminck, Fauvist painter (Village in the Snow), born in Paris, France (d. 1958)
Gabriel Grovlez, French pianist, conductor (Opéra de Paris, 1914-34), and composer (L'Almanach aux images), born in Lille, France (d. 1944)
Georg von Trapp, Austrian WWI submarine commander and inspiration for "The Sound of Music" character, born in Zara, Kingdom of Dalmatia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1947)
Charles Earle Funk, American civil engineer, lexicographer and editor (Funk & Wagnalls, New International Year Book), born in Springfield, Ohio (d. 1957)[1]
Mary Howe, American composer (Sand) and pianist, born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 1964)
William Cumming Rose, American biochemist who discovered threonine (an amino acid), born in Greenville, South Carolina (d. 1985)
Zdzisław Żygulski, Sr., Polish literary historian, born in Przemyśl, Poland (d. 1975)
Cyril Smith, Scottish actor (The Adventures of Sir Lancelot), born in Peterhead, Scotland (d. 1963)
Esther Howard, American actress, born in Helena, Montana (d. 1965)
Hans Riegel Sr., German confectioner and inventor of  the 'gummy bear' (founder of Haribo), born in Bonn, Germany (d. 1945)
Ruth Cummings (née Sinclair), American stage, silent and sound screen actress (Daybreak; Dream of Love), and screenwriter, born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1984)
Arthur Murray [Moses Teichman], American ballroom dancer and businessman (Arthur Murray Dance Party), born in New York City (d. 1991)
Benjamin Thackston "Tack" Knight, American cartoonist ("Little Folks"), born in Dillsboro, North Carolina (d. 1976)
Charlie Hallows, English cricket batsman (Lancs played for England 1921-28), born in Little Lever, Lancashire (d. 1972)
Robert Sherwood, American dramatist (Abe Lincoln in Illinois; Idiot's Delight), born in New Rochelle, New York (d. 1955)
Tristan Tzara [Samuel Rosenfeld] Romanian-French poet (Approximate Man), born in Moinești, Romania (d. 1963)
Pierre Fresnay, French actor (Monsieur Vincent, Grand Illusion), born in Paris, France (d. 1975)
Agnes Ayres [Hinkle], American actress (Sheik, Souls at Sea), born in Carbondale, Illinois (d. 1940)
Carmel Myers, American stage and silent and sound screen actress (Ben-Hur; Svengali), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1980)
William Brann, South African cricketer (South African batsman v England 1922-23), born in Port Elizabeth, Cape Province (d. 1953)
Louise Lévêque de Vilmorin, French poet and author (Lutin Sauvage), born in Verrières-le-Buisson, Essonne, Paris (d. 1969)
Stanley G. Weinbaum, American sci-fi author (Martian Odyssey), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1935)
Eugene Bozza, French composer, born in Nice, France (d. 1991)
Tony Manero, American golfer (US Open 1936), born in New York City (d. 1989)

Bea Benaderet, American radio and screen comic actress (Petticoat Junction - "Kate"; The Beverly Hillbillies - "Cousin Pearl"; George Burns and Gracie Allen Show - "Blanche"), born in New York City (d. 1968)
John Cameron Swayze, American newscaster (The Hindenberg), and spokesperson (Timex), born in Wichita, Kansas (d. 1995)
Nathan M. Pusey, American educator, 24th president of Harvard University (1953-71), born in Council Bluffs, Iowa (d. 2001)
Ernestine Gilbreth Carey, American author (Cheaper by the Dozen), born in New York City (d. 2006)
Barthélemy Boganda, French-African politician (Central African Republic's 1st President, 1958-59), born in Bobangui, French Colony of Ubangi-Shari (d. 1959)
Dave Brown, Australian rugby league centre (9 Tests, 19 games NSW; Eastern Suburbs, Warrington; RL "Immortal") and coach (Eastern Suburbs), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1974)

Frances Langford, American singer (I'm In The Mood For Love), entertainer (Armed Forces Hour; USO tours), actress (All-American Co-Ed; Born To Dance), and philanthropist, born in Hernando, Florida (d. 2005)
Jerome Weidman, American writer (Tenderloin), born in New York City (d. 1998)

John Beith, British diplomat who served as the ambassador to Israel and Belgium, born in England (d. 2000)[1]
Marguerite Duras, French novelist and playwright (Sea Wall), born in Saigon, French IndoChina (d. 1996)
Jan Drda, Czech writer (Nema Barikada, Mestecko Na Dlani), born in Příbram, Czech Republic (d. 1970)
Lars Ahlin, Swedish writer (My Death is My Own), born in Sundsvall, Sweden (d. 1997)
David White, American actor (Larry Tate-Bewitched), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1990)
Mickey Owen, American baseball catcher (4 × MLB All-Star 1941–44 Brooklyn Dodgers), born in Nixa, Missouri (d. 2005)
Nikola Ljubičić, President of the Presidency of Serbia (1982-84) and Communist politician, born in Karan, Užice, Kingdom of Serbia (d. 2005)
Joseph Ortiz, French-Algerian extremist and rebel (barricade uprising), born in Guyotville, Algeria (d. 1995)
George Jellicoe, Earl Jellicoe, British naval commander and member of the House of Lords (1939-2007), born in Hatfield (d. 2007)
Antony Tudor, English dancer and choreographer (Metropolitan Opera 1957), born in London (d. 1987)
Éric Rohmer [Maurice Schérer], French director, journalist and teacher (My Night at Maud's, Claire's Knee, Le rayon vert), born in Tulle, Corrèze, France (d. 2010)
Elmer Bernstein, American film music composer (The Magnificent Seven; To Kill A Mockingbird; The Age Of Innocence; Thoroughly Modern Millie), born in New York City (d. 2004)
Gene Reynolds, American screenwriter, director, and producer (M*A*S*H), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2020)
Manon Alving, Dutch actress (The Assault, Short American), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 2010)
Peter Vaughan, British actor (Porridge, Game of Thrones), born in Wem, Shropshire, England (d. 2016)
Chris Prater, English screenprinter, born in London (d. 1996)
Paul Ornstein, Hungarian-American Holocaust survivor and psychoanalyst, born in Hajdunanas, Hungary (d. 2017)
(Huberdina) "Erna" Spoorenberg, Dutch soprano (Vienna State Opera, De Nederlandse Opera), and voice teacher, born in Yogyakarta, Java, Dutch East Indies (now Indonesia) (d. 2004)
Claude Wagner, French Canadian politician and judge, born in Shawinigan, Quebec, Canada (d. 1979)
Elizabeth Wilson, American screen and stage actress (Doc, East Side/West Side), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 2015)
Emmett Williams, American poet, born in Greenville, South Carolina (d. 2007)
Mother Divine [Edna Rose Ritchings], cult leader of the International Peace Mission Movement, born in Vancouver (d. 2017)
Bob Stump, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from Arizona, born in Phoenix, Arizona (d. 2003)
Alfredo "Chocolate" Armenteros, Cuban jazz trumpeter (Orchestra Harlow; Eddie Palmieri), born in Santa Clara, Cuba (d. 2016)
Bill Ryan, American newscaster (Smithsonian), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1997)
Estelle Harris, American actress and comedian (Estelle Costanza - Seinfeld; Toy Story), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2022)
Jimmy Logan, British comedian (Mad Death), born in Dennistoun, Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2001)
Monty Norman [Noserovitch], British big band singer, songwriter (False Hearted Lover), and film composer (James Bond Theme), born in Stepney, England (d. 2022)
Patricia McBride Lousada, American ballerina (Ballet Society/ New York City Ballet, 1946-51), and cookbook author (Pasta Italian Style), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2019)
William F. Clinger Jr., American politician and Republican Representative from Pennsylvania (1979-97), born in Warren, Pennsylvania (d. 2021)
Bobby Ray Inman, American admiral and intelligence director, born in Rhonesboro, Texas
Denis Frank Owen, British ecologist and natural historian, born in London (d. 1996)
Harold Volkmer, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from Missouri, born in Jefferson City, Missouri (d. 2011)
Bapu Nadkarni, Indian cricket all-rounder (41 Tests, top score 122; 88 wickets), born in Nashik, India (d. 2020)
Clive Davis, American record producer and music industry executive (Columbia, Arista, Sony), born in Brooklyn, New York City
Pierre Lacotte, French choreographer and ballet historian, born in Chatou, France (d. 2023)[1]
